title: Unexpected Provisioning Package
description: Detects provisioning packages (PPKG) that are not on the built-in allow-list. The agent emits one or more provisioning_package_scan events whose artifacts[] arrays list every PPKG (registry package, .ppkg file, or Recovery\Customizations residue; a large set is split across several events). This rule iterates that array across all of them and fires for any artifact whose identity does not match the allow-list regex. The default allow-list covers the Windows OS-inbox packages (Cosa, Power.EnergyEstimationEngine, Power.Settings, SecureStart) plus common OEM factory-preload families (Intel PPM power profiles, AMD Power profiles, Lenovo ThinkPad power/battery packages, Microsoft Surface defaults and carrier packages) so clean factory devices stay quiet. OEM imaging leftovers (e.g. USMT.PPKG, InstallOrder.ini) and generically named packages deliberately still alert — on a pure-Autopilot device they are worth a look. To allow your own vendor/recovery packages, enable the template rule ANALYZE-SEC-006, extend its allow-list, then disable this rule.
explanation:
A provisioning package (PPKG) was detected that is NOT on the allow-list.

- **Unexpected package:** `{{identity}}`

The agent reports every PPKG it finds in `provisioning_package_scan` event(s) (see their `artifacts` array(s) for the full list, plus file metadata and best-effort content indicators; large sets span several events). This rule ignores the Windows OS-inbox packages (Cosa, Power.EnergyEstimationEngine, Power.Settings, SecureStart) and the common OEM factory-preload families (Intel PPM power profiles, AMD Power profiles, Lenovo ThinkPad power/battery packages, Surface defaults and carrier packages) and only surfaces the rest. OEM imaging leftovers such as `USMT.PPKG` or `InstallOrder.ini` are deliberately NOT allow-listed — on a device expected to be freshly provisioned via Autopilot they indicate the image was touched by another deployment workflow.

Provisioning packages can apply local accounts, certificates, Wi-Fi/VPN profiles, applications (MSI/EXE) and scripts. They are legitimate in bulk-enrollment and OEM-recovery scenarios, but a PPKG appearing on a device that is expected to be "pure Autopilot" can indicate tampering.

**To stop alerting on a known-good package:** enable the template rule **ANALYZE-SEC-006**, add the package to its allow-list regex, then disable this rule. ANALYZE-SEC-006 then only fires for packages outside your extended allow-list.

remediation.title: Verify the provisioning package is expected
steps: Open the provisioning_package_scan event and review the artifacts array (identity, source, file path, ownerType/installTime for registry packages)
Confirm whether the package matches a known source: bulk-enrollment PPKG, OEM recovery package, or an internal provisioning workflow
Cross-check the device model/vendor — OEM recovery PPKGs are common and usually benign
If the package is unexpected for a pure-Autopilot device, treat it as a potential manipulation and investigate how it was applied (USB, Recovery\Customizations, image)
To allow known-good packages going forward, enable ANALYZE-SEC-006, extend the allow-list, then disable this rule
